text:
The Trivalent Actinide Lanthanide Separation with Phosphorus-reagent Extraction from Aqueous Komplexes (TALSPEAK) process, developed in the 1960s at Oak Ridge National Laboratory, remains a primary benchmark for the partitioning of trivalent f-elements. By utilizing the selective extraction of lanthanides into an organic phase (typically HDEHP) while retaining actinides in an aqueous phase via aminopolycarboxylate complexants (primarily DTPA), the process achieves significant separation factors. Despite its efficiency, large-scale industrial adoption has been hindered by rigorous pH requirements, high buffer concentrations, and suboptimal phase transfer kinetics.

This review synthesizes a decade of fundamental scientific advancements aimed at resolving these long-standing operational challenges. Recent thermodynamic modeling and experimental investigations reveal that the process’s steep pH dependence is largely driven by the independent partitioning of lactic acid and sodium into the organic phase, which competitively inhibits the extractant. Molecular-scale studies—including FTIR, NMR, and Small Angle Neutron Scattering (SANS)—have clarified the speciation of extracted complexes, identifying a transition from monomeric 1:6 metal-extractant species to polynuclear aggregated clusters under high loading conditions. Furthermore, aqueous kinetic studies suggest that lactate acts as a phase transfer catalyst by facilitating the formation of transient, hydrophobic tris-lactate species at the interface. These fundamental insights have led to the development of "Advanced TALSPEAK" concepts, which utilize alternative extractants like HEH[EHP] and weaker complexants such as HEDTA to achieve more robust pH stability and faster kinetics. This report provides a comprehensive overview of the chemical interactions and supramolecular organization that govern this essential separation chemistry.
